props.label: At a Glance · From the Tape Desk

bullets.text: The Bank of Korea raised its Base Rate to 2.75%, its first increase in about 3½ years. Semiconductor-led exports strengthened the case as the won weakened about 3.4%.
The KOSPI fell 6.37% to 6,820.60, with SK Hynix’s Seoul shares down 11.5%. Its US ADR traced a five-session path of $152 → $194 → $154.
Brent settled at $88.10, up about 4.6%, while WTI reached $82.49. Hormuz traffic running near a tenth of normal levels kept the strait’s disruption premium embedded in crude.

props.caption: The Bank of Korea wrote semiconductors into its case for the first rate rise in about three and a half years — the AI-compute cycle reaching from server racks into a central bank’s reaction function.

props.title: The Tape
props.kicker: 17 Jul close · KOSPI 16 Jul · crude settle

items.why: Kickoff resolves the outstanding calls on a possible FIFA charge, a security incident and final-day air quality around the stadium.

items.what: US 25% tariff on Brazilian goods takes effect
items.why: Brasília must decide whether to invoke its Reciprocity Law, extend negotiations or continue cushioning affected exporters without immediate retaliation.

items.what: Another acknowledged strike on Gulf civilian infrastructure
items.why: A second confirmed hit on power generation or water supply would establish a fresh escalation threshold for the regional conflict.
A settlement at or above $90 would confirm that disrupted Hormuz traffic has produced a durable risk premium in crude.
A disclosed shot from a second vendor would show that the collaborative combat-aircraft programme is developing across a fleet of competing systems.

items.what: A second US state datacenter moratorium
items.why: Another statewide pause following New York would turn local grid, land and permitting disputes into a broader national constraint on AI infrastructure.

props.meta: Calibrated calls · 2026-07-18 · Tinkerton, Sprockett, Graves, Foreman, Cogsworth · two prior calls resolved this edition

open_calls.question: FIFA issues a formal disciplinary charge or sanction against the Argentine federation over the Malvinas banner before the final
open_calls.detail: The disciplinary committee says it is still evaluating the match reports; the comparable 2014 fine for the same slogan landed after that tournament, so a charge before Sunday’s kickoff is less likely than one after it.

open_calls.question: The Spain–Argentina final is played without a security incident causing reported injuries or arrests at MetLife
open_calls.detail: The final is rated SEAR 1 and wrapped in a $625m federal security-grant architecture with a protected head of state expected; the base rate for a major incident at such an event is low, though the target profile is high.

open_calls.question: Northeastern New Jersey records an air-quality index of 101 or higher during the final
open_calls.detail: Canadian wildfire smoke pushed the metro AQI near 148 on 18 July, but a drier, less humid Sunday is forecast to move the plume offshore; no official Sunday reading exists yet and residual haze remains possible.

open_calls.question: Iran or an Iran-aligned actor conducts another officially acknowledged strike on critical water or power infrastructure in a GCC state
open_calls.detail: A Kuwaiti power-and-desalination station was reported hit as U.S. strikes entered their seventh night; with the campaign still running and no ceasefire in sight, a second acknowledged infrastructure hit is more likely than not.

open_calls.question: Front-month Brent crude settles at or above $90 a barrel
open_calls.detail: Brent settled at $88.10 on Friday, up about 4.6% on the hostilities, but a settle above $90 needs a further leg that rerouting and ship-to-ship transfers off Oman have so far absorbed.

open_calls.question: A second U.S. state imposes a statewide moratorium or formal permit pause on new hyperscale datacenters
open_calls.detail: New York’s Executive Order 62 is the first statewide pause; imitation is plausible given a national protest wave, but most states are still competing to attract AI investment rather than pause it.

items.what: SK Hynix’s US ADR closed 17 July at $154.03 after a violent week that carried the shares from roughly $152 to $194 and back. The AI-memory trade ended the week still searching for a stable level.

Brent and WTI rose about 4.5% on Friday as US–Iran hostilities intensified. Fears of a Red Sea closure added another potential choke point to an energy market already pricing disruption through Hormuz.
The Korean won weakened about 3.4% against the dollar toward 1,480. The currency move added pressure on the Bank of Korea as it raised rates amid strong semiconductor exports.
